summary: Gruber argues that Nvidia has inherited the crown of tech keynotes from Apple, citing Jensen Huang's CES 2025 keynote which drew over 6,000 enthusiastic attendees in an arena setting resembling a rock concert. He reflects on Apple's decision to abandon live keynotes in favor of pre-filmed presentations, acknowledging the trade-offs: polished production and higher viewership versus the irreplaceable magic of a live audience. Gruber draws parallels to Taylor Swift's Eras tour and live sports to argue that shared live experiences create something video cannot replicate. He notes that Apple once commanded outsized attention for its keynotes — even rivaling all of CES — but voluntarily walked away from that power. While praising Huang as charismatic, Gruber observes that Nvidia's keynote lacked the obsessive rehearsal that defined Apple's live era, invoking Steve Jobs and Penn & Teller's philosophy that the real secret is putting in unreasonable amounts of work.
key_points: Nvidia's CES 2025 keynote drew 6,000+ attendees in an arena, resembling a rock concert more than a tech event
Apple voluntarily abandoned live keynotes despite owning that space in tech for decades, and no pre-filmed format can replicate the magic of a live audience
The most famous keynote in history — the iPhone introduction — was at Macworld 2007, and Apple once singlehandedly rivaled CES for attention
Jensen Huang is charismatic but Nvidia didn't rehearse nearly as much as Apple did under Jobs, who treated rehearsal as the real secret to great presentations
Apple's pre-filmed keynotes get higher viewership than ever, but viewership numbers and live audience energy are fundamentally different things
money_quotes: It resembled a rock concert more than a tech keynote.
There's magic in a live audience. It just matters.
Attending a live sporting event is profoundly different than watching on TV.
A live movie-watching event is not a live performance.
There's clearly a new answer to the question of which company holds the keynotes which attract the most enthusiastic large live audiences: Nvidia.
Nobody remembers anything at all from the CES 2007 keynote, which Bill Gates delivered the next day in Las Vegas. I'll bet Bill Gates himself doesn't remember anything from that keynote.
Sometimes magic is just someone spending more time on something than anyone else might reasonably expect.
They don't just work a little bit harder on them than most companies. They work way harder than any other company, or CEO, would reasonably expect.
key_insight: Apple voluntarily ceded the crown of live tech keynotes by switching to pre-filmed presentations, and Nvidia — fueled by genuinely exciting products and an enthusiastic audience — has stepped into that void.
